title: A Canadian Freelancer's Guide to Setting Up a US Stripe Account

interlinking_suggestions.title: Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam: The Ultimate Digital Nomad Guide (2025)
How to Open a Stripe Account for a Non-US Business

author.name: Samuel Chen
author.bio: A former product manager at a major fintech company, Samuel has deep expertise in the global payments landscape. He analyzes financial tools and strategies to help freelancers maximize their earnings and minimize fees.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Avoid Stripe's default 2% currency conversion fee by opening a USD-denominated account at your Canadian bank and setting it as your primary payout destination.
Recognize that forming a U.S. LLC as a Canadian creates a non-negotiable annual IRS filing requirement (Form 5472) with a severe, automatic $25,000 penalty for failure to file.
Budget for a minimum of $2,500-$5,000+ in annual administrative costs for a U.S. LLC, driven primarily by the non-negotiable need for a cross-border accountant.
Delay establishing a U.S. LLC until your U.S. revenue exceeds $150,000 annually, as the compliance costs and risks typically outweigh the benefits below this threshold.
summary: Canadian freelancers often lose significant profit to Stripe's automatic currency conversion fees, while the common advice to form a U.S. LLC introduces high costs and severe compliance risks. The article's core advice is to first optimize your existing Canadian setup by opening a USD bank account in Canada to receive and hold U.S. dollars directly. This simple strategy provides the key outcome of immediately protecting your profit margins and gaining full control over your USD revenue with the lowest possible administrative burden.
ai_overview_answer:
For Canadian freelancers, the most profitable way to accept USD payments is to reconfigure their existing Canadian Stripe account to avoid automatic currency conversion fees. This is achieved by opening a USD-denominated business account at a major Canadian bank, such as RBC, TD, or BMO. Once opened, link this new USD account to your Stripe dashboard and set it as the default payout destination for all USD-based revenue. This simple change stops Stripe's automatic 2% currency conversion fee, giving you full control over when and how you convert your funds.

For businesses with over $150,000 in U.S. revenue or those facing significant payment friction, establishing a U.S. LLC and a U.S. Stripe account is a more advanced strategy. However, this path carries significant compliance costs and risks. It creates a non-negotiable requirement to file Form 5472 with the IRS annually, even if no tax is owed. Failure to file this form results in a severe, automatic $25,000 penalty, making the optimized Canadian setup the safest and most profitable choice for the majority of freelancers.
disclaimer: The information provided in this article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or financial advice. You should consult with a qualified professional for advice tailored to your individual situation.

itemListElement.name: A Canadian Freelancer's Guide to Setting Up a US Stripe Account

@graph.name: How to Set Up a US Stripe Account as a Canadian Freelancer
@graph.description: A guide for Canadian freelancers on the two primary methods for accepting USD payments: optimizing a Canadian Stripe account or establishing a full U.S. LLC and U.S. Stripe account.

step.name: The Default Path: Mastering USD Payments in Canada

itemListElement.name: Open a USD-Denominated Business Account
itemListElement.text: Approach your existing Canadian business bank and ask to open a USD business chequing account. This is a standard product offering and is the foundation of your cross-border payment infrastructure.

itemListElement.name: Link Your New USD Account to Stripe
itemListElement.text: Navigate to your Stripe Dashboard. Under Settings > Business Settings, find the section for External payout accounts. Add the details of your new Canadian USD account here.

itemListElement.name: Set Your USD Account as the Payout Default
itemListElement.text: Once verified, designate this account as the destination for all payouts originating in USD. Now, when a U.S. client pays your invoice, the funds (less Stripe's processing fee) will land in your account as USD.

step.name: The Power Play: Establishing a U.S. Presence

itemListElement.name: Form Your U.S. Entity
itemListElement.text: Create a legal entity within the United States, typically a Wyoming or Delaware LLC. You must appoint a Registered Agent to provide a physical address in your state of formation.

itemListElement.name: Secure Your Employer Identification Number (EIN)
itemListElement.text: Obtain an EIN from the IRS by filing Form SS-4. As a non-resident, you do not need an SSN or ITIN for this process.

itemListElement.name: Build Your U.S. Financial Stack
itemListElement.text: Acquire a U.S. physical mailing address (virtual mailbox), a U.S. phone number (VOIP service), and open a U.S. business bank account with a fintech platform like Mercury or Relay Financial.

mainEntity.name: What are the U.S. tax filing requirements for a Canadian-owned LLC?

acceptedAnswer.text: You have a non-negotiable annual requirement to file Form 5472 with the IRS, even if your LLC owes no tax. This is an informational return disclosing transactions between your LLC and you. The penalty for failure to file is a staggering $25,000. If your activities are considered "Engaged in a Trade or Business in the United States" (ETBUS), you may also have to file a U.S. tax return (e.g., Form 1040-NR) and pay U.S. taxes. This complexity makes a cross-border accountant essential.

mainEntity.name: Can a Canadian freelancer avoid Stripe's 2% currency conversion fee?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. The most direct and lowest-risk method is to optimize your existing Canadian Stripe account. Open a USD-denominated business bank account at a major Canadian bank and link it to your Stripe profile. Stripe will then deposit your U.S. client payments in USD directly, giving you full control over currency conversion.

mainEntity.name: Can a non-resident open a U.S. business bank account online?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. While most traditional U.S. banks require an in-person visit, digital banking platforms like Mercury and Relay Financial are designed for non-resident founders. With your U.S. LLC formation documents and your Employer Identification Number (EIN), you can open a fully functional U.S. business bank account remotely.

mainEntity.name: Do I need an SSN or ITIN to open a U.S. Stripe account for my LLC?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. As the non-resident owner of a U.S. LLC, your business's identity for all financial and tax purposes is its Employer Identification Number (EIN). You do not need a personal U.S. tax ID like a Social Security Number (SSN) or Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN).

mainEntity.name: What's the difference between Stripe Atlas and forming an LLC myself?

acceptedAnswer.text: Stripe Atlas is a bundled service that often defaults to creating a Delaware C-Corporation, a structure with more rigid formalities and complex tax implications that are generally unsuitable for a solo freelancer. Forming an LLC yourself through an online service gives you the freedom to choose your state (like Wyoming) and select the entity type that best fits your simpler operational needs.

body.content: The Default Path to Profit: Mastering USD Payments with Your Canadian Stripe Account

For Canadian freelancers serving U.S. clients, the path to maximizing profit is often obscured by complex advice about U.S. corporate structures. The truth is simpler: the most profitable first move is to perfect the powerful tools you already have access to in Canada. This strategy allows you to manage U.S. revenue with the lowest possible compliance burden and maximum control.

The core objective is to get paid in USD and hold it as USD. This tactic is your primary defense against the automatic, costly currency conversions that erode your hard-earned revenue. A standard Canadian Stripe account, by default, converts all USD payments into CAD upon payout, levying a 2% currency conversion fee on top of standard processing fees. This seemingly small percentage quietly compounds, slicing a significant portion from your top line. By taking direct control, you stop this automatic fee erosion.
The Default Path: Mastering USD Payments in Canada

The solution is not to stop using your Canadian Stripe account, but to reconfigure it. You can prevent forced currency conversions by opening a USD-denominated business account at a major Canadian bank. Institutions like RBC, TD, and BMO all offer these accounts, giving you a place to receive and hold U.S. dollars *as* U.S. dollars, all within the familiar Canadian banking system.
Here is the step-by-step playbook:

items: **Open a USD-Denominated Business Account.** Approach your existing Canadian business bank and ask to open a USD business chequing account. This is a standard product offering and is the foundation of your cross-border payment infrastructure.

**Link Your New USD Account to Stripe.** Navigate to your Stripe Dashboard. Under `Settings > Business Settings`, find the section for `External payout accounts`. Add the details of your new Canadian USD account here.

**Set Your USD Account as the Payout Default.** Once verified, designate this account as the destination for all payouts originating in USD. Now, when a U.S. client pays your invoice, the funds (less Stripe's processing fee) will land in your account as USD.

body.content: With this structure, you unplug the forced conversion machine. You now hold a balance of U.S. dollars, giving you complete authority over when and how you convert your funds. You can wait for a favorable exchange rate or use a service like Wise to move funds to your CAD account at a much lower conversion rate, often under 1%. This single strategic shift directly protects your profit margins.

However, this path has its limitations. Because your banking infrastructure is still Canadian, some U.S. clients' credit card issuers may flag transactions as "international," which can lead to slightly higher payment failure rates. Furthermore, some large corporate clients may prefer or require paying into a U.S.-domiciled bank account. When these limitations become consistent barriers to growth, it’s time to consider the next level.
The Power Play: When and How to Establish a U.S. Presence

When payment friction becomes a tangible barrier, it’s a clear signal you may have outgrown the Canadian-only approach. This is the time to consider the power play: establishing a genuine U.S. presence through a Limited Liability Company (LLC) and a U.S. Stripe account. This path trades simplicity for power, but it must be navigated with precision.
Here is the operational checklist to build your U.S. financial infrastructure:
1. Form Your U.S. Entity

First, you must create a legal entity within the United States. For most non-residents, an LLC is the preferred structure. **Wyoming** and **Delaware** are popular choices; Wyoming offers enhanced privacy and low fees, while Delaware has a robust legal system favored by companies seeking future investment. As part of this process, you must appoint a **Registered Agent**, a mandatory service that provides a physical address in your state of formation to receive official correspondence.
2. Secure Your Link to the IRS: The EIN

With your LLC formed, you must obtain an **Employer Identification Number (EIN)** from the IRS. This is a unique identifier for your business for all tax and banking matters. Crucially, as a non-resident, you **do not need** a Social Security Number (SSN) or Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN) to get one. The process involves filing Form SS-4 with the IRS by fax or mail.
3. Build Your U.S. Financial Stack
Your LLC and EIN are the keys to unlocking the U.S. financial system. You will need:

items: **A U.S. Physical Mailing Address:** A virtual mailbox service easily fulfills this requirement.
**A U.S. Phone Number:** A VOIP service provides a legitimate U.S. phone number for verification.

**A U.S. Business Bank Account:** This is the most critical component. While traditional banks often require an in-person visit, modern fintech platforms like **Mercury** and **Relay Financial** are built for non-resident founders and allow remote account opening.

body.content: With this complete infrastructure, you can confidently apply for a U.S. Stripe account, linking it to your U.S. bank account and EIN. This structure can significantly lower payment failure rates and enhance your credibility with large corporate clients.
The Compliance Trap: The Hidden Costs of a U.S. LLC

These strategic advantages are not free. They are purchased with a binding commitment to a complex compliance regime that many online formation services conveniently gloss over. Opening a U.S. LLC as a Canadian is a formal relationship with the Internal Revenue Service (IRS), and ignoring this is the single biggest risk you can take.

items: **The Non-Negotiable Filing: Form 5472.** If you are a Canadian who owns a U.S. single-member LLC, you are required to file Form 5472 with the IRS annually. This is an *informational return* to report transactions between you and your LLC (like paying yourself). It must be filed even if your LLC made no money and you owe zero U.S. tax. The penalty for failing to file, filing late, or filing incorrectly is a severe, automatic **$25,000 USD penalty**.

**Understanding Your U.S. Tax Nexus.** You must determine if your business activities create a U.S. tax obligation. This hinges on whether you are considered "**Engaged in a Trade or Business in the United States**" (ETBUS). This status is generally triggered if you have a "dependent agent" (like an employee or exclusive contractor) in the U.S. performing substantial work for your business. If you are deemed ETBUS, you will be required to file a U.S. federal tax return, such as Form 1040-NR.

**The Peril of Double Taxation.** As a Canadian resident, you are taxed on your worldwide income. The U.S. and Canada view LLCs differently, which can create significant tax complications. Without meticulous planning by a qualified cross-border accountant, you can easily find yourself taxed on the same income by both the IRS and the CRA.
**Quantifying the Operational Overhead.** Beyond tax risk, a U.S. LLC carries real, quantifiable annual costs. You must budget for this new operational overhead.

rows: Mandatory service required in your state of formation.
Wyoming has a ~$60 annual report fee; Delaware charges a $300 flat franchise tax.
Essential for navigating Form 5472, ETBUS considerations, and the complex interaction between U.S. and Canadian tax law to avoid costly errors. This is non-negotiable.
This is the minimum baseline cost to remain in good standing *before* paying any actual tax.

body.content: The Decision Framework: Is a U.S. LLC Worth the Cost?

Staring at a potential annual bill of over $5,000 just for administration leads to the critical question: when does this investment generate a positive return? As the CEO of your business, you need a clear framework to make a confident decision.

items:
**Tier 1: Under $50k in Annual U.S. Revenue.**
The verdict is clear: **the compliance cost and risk of a U.S. LLC far outweigh any potential benefits.** The administrative overhead could consume more than 10% of your gross U.S. revenue. Your focus should be 100% on optimizing the Canadian USD Account path.

**Tier 2: $50k - $150k in Annual U.S. Revenue.**
This is the grey zone where the decision becomes more about strategy than pure math. An LLC is a **"maybe,"** and the deciding factor is often **client concentration**. If you have one or two large U.S. corporate clients who have expressed difficulty with international payments, the cost may be justified as a client retention strategy.

**Tier 3: Over $150k+ in Annual U.S. Revenue.**
The strategic case for a U.S. LLC becomes compelling. At this volume, the administrative overhead is a manageable percentage of revenue. More importantly, reducing a high credit card failure rate on a six-figure income stream adds real dollars to your bottom line. You have the cash flow to properly support the expert accounting required, transforming the LLC from a defensive cost into an offensive tool for growth.

body.content: The Ultimate Litmus Test: The "Access" Question

Beyond revenue, the final deciding factor is access. Ask yourself this: **Do you critically need access to U.S.-only financial products to enable the next phase of your business growth?** This could mean specific payment gateways, U.S. business credit cards, or positioning your business for future U.S. investment. If the answer is a firm "yes," the U.S. LLC transitions from a high-cost option to an essential piece of strategic infrastructure, regardless of your current revenue.
Frequently Asked Questions
What are the U.S. tax filing requirements for a Canadian-owned LLC?

You have a non-negotiable annual requirement to file Form 5472 with the IRS, even if your LLC owes no tax. This is an informational return disclosing transactions between your LLC and you. The penalty for failure to file is a staggering **$25,000**. If your activities are considered "Engaged in a Trade or Business in the United States" (ETBUS), you may also have to file a U.S. tax return (e.g., Form 1040-NR) and pay U.S. taxes. This complexity makes a cross-border accountant essential.
Can a Canadian freelancer avoid Stripe's 2% currency conversion fee?

Yes. The most direct and lowest-risk method is to optimize your existing Canadian Stripe account. Open a USD-denominated business bank account at a major Canadian bank and link it to your Stripe profile. Stripe will then deposit your U.S. client payments in USD directly, giving you full control over currency conversion.
Can a non-resident open a U.S. business bank account online?

Yes. While most traditional U.S. banks require an in-person visit, digital banking platforms like Mercury and Relay Financial are designed for non-resident founders. With your U.S. LLC formation documents and your Employer Identification Number (EIN), you can open a fully functional U.S. business bank account remotely.
Do I need an SSN or ITIN to open a U.S. Stripe account for my LLC?

No. As the non-resident owner of a U.S. LLC, your business's identity for all financial and tax purposes is its Employer Identification Number (EIN). You do not need a personal U.S. tax ID like a Social Security Number (SSN) or Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN).
What's the difference between Stripe Atlas and forming an LLC myself?

Stripe Atlas is a bundled service that often defaults to creating a Delaware C-Corporation, a structure with more rigid formalities and complex tax implications that are generally unsuitable for a solo freelancer. Forming an LLC yourself through an online service gives you the freedom to choose your state (like Wyoming) and select the entity type that best fits your simpler operational needs.
Conclusion: Choose Control, Not Complexity

Ultimately, your U.S. payment structure is a direct reflection of your business strategy. The choice is between operational simplicity with profit protection, or aggressive U.S. market expansion with its attendant costs and risks.

For the vast majority of Canadian freelancers, the conclusion is unambiguous. The most profitable and secure path is to perfect your existing Canadian Stripe infrastructure by linking it to a Canadian-domiciled USD bank account. This single maneuver eliminates forced currency conversion fees and gives you complete control over your USD revenue. It delivers the lion's share of the benefits with a fraction of the risk, keeping your operations firmly in Canada, where you understand the rules.

A U.S. LLC and its corresponding U.S. Stripe account should be viewed not as a default next step, but as a precision instrument for a specific and compelling purpose. The moment you form that entity, you are irrevocably bound to its compliance obligations. The annual upkeep is a substantial financial and operational drag that must be justified by a clear strategic return.
To make this choice tangible, consider this direct comparison:

header: Path B: U.S. LLC & U.S. Stripe Account
rows: Control over USD; avoidance of conversion fees.
Access to U.S.-only financial tools; enhanced U.S. client credibility.
Severe IRS penalties for missed filings (Form 5472); potential for double taxation.
Freelancers focused on profit margin, simplicity, and risk mitigation.
High-growth freelancers needing U.S. financial products or facing significant U.S. client payment friction.

body.content: The decision rests on a sober assessment of your business. Is the friction of U.S. payment failures costing you more than the thousands you will spend on compliance? Do you have an undeniable need for access to U.S.-only financial tools to unlock your next phase of growth? If the answer is not a resounding "yes," then the choice is simple. Choose the framework that maximizes your control, minimizes your compliance risk, and protects your peace of mind.
